full_response:
Warm-up  
Property: “every clause contains exactly one literal’’ (a 1-CNF formula).  
Instance looks like  
(x₇) ∧ (¬x₃) ∧ (x₂) ∧ (x₃) ∧ (¬x₂) …

------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Observation
A 1-CNF formula F is a conjunction of unit literals.  
To satisfy F every listed literal must be made true.  
Hence F is satisfiable ⇔ no variable is required to be simultaneously true and false.

------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Polynomial-time algorithm

Input: list of m unit clauses over variables x₁,…,x_n.

Pseudocode
------------------------------------------------------------
Unit-SAT(F)
    create empty dictionary forcedValue     // key: variable id, value: {T,F}
    for each literal ℓ in F do
        v ← variable(ℓ)
        val ← (ℓ is positive) ? T : F
        if v ∈ forcedValue and forcedValue[v] ≠ val then
             return “UNSAT”                 // conflicting requirement
        forcedValue[v] ← val
    return “SAT” together with assignment:
           set each recorded variable to its forced value,
           all other variables arbitrary
------------------------------------------------------------

Running time: we scan each of the m literals once ⇒ Θ(m).  
Space: O(n) to store at most one value per variable.

------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Proof of correctness   (direct argument)

•  If the algorithm outputs UNSAT, it found some variable x that
   must be true and false simultaneously. Any assignment violates
   at least one of these two clauses, so F is indeed unsatisfiable.

•  If the algorithm outputs SAT, every variable receives at most one
   value, hence every literal in F is satisfied by the constructed
   assignment; conjunction of satisfied literals ⇒ F is satisfied.

Thus the algorithm is correct.

------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Complexity class implication
Because Unit-SAT runs in Θ(m) time, the special case “CNF-SAT where every
clause has exactly one literal’’ lies in P.  There is no contradiction
with the assumption P ≠ NP, because the general CNF-SAT remains NP-complete.

\boxed{\text{1-CNF (all clauses length 1) is solvable in linear time; hence the claimed polynomial-time algorithm is possible.}}

Confidence: 0.51
extracted_answer: \text{1-CNF (all clauses length 1) is solvable in linear time; hence the claimed polynomial-time algorithm is possible.}
